Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative fixed clause; modal verb 'nyalo' + infinitive verb; metaphorical subject construction
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Time reveals hidden truths or eventually exposes wrongdoing
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wach ma opando ochopo e ler bangʼe
Usage Contexts: Politics, family disputes, leadership conflicts, moral discussions
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in agrarian worldview where daylight exposes what darkness hides; common among elders in Nyanza
First Example Sentence: Chiengʼ nyalo podho piny, koro wabiro ngʼeyo ngʼa ma ne owacho adiera.
Second Example Sentence: Ok icham gi dwaro mar piny; chiengʼ nyalo podho piny bangʼe.
Third Example Sentence: Ne gichako loko weche e piny, to chiengʼ nyalo podho piny e ndalo machiegni.

Idiom (Dholuo): Ngʼato ok nyal pimo pi gi lwet achiel
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative clause; modal verb + infinitive; instrumental phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One person cannot accomplish everything alone
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ngʼato achiel ok nyal timo duto ka onge kony
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects communal labor traditions like harambee and cooperative fishing practices
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Dholuo): [ŋàtɔ̀ ɔ̀k ɲàl pìmò pì gí lwɛ̀t àt͡ʃíɛ̀l]
First Example Sentence: Wach mar puothe omiyo waneno ni ngʼato ok nyal pimo pi gi lwet achiel.
Second Example Sentence: Ka idwaro teko maduongʼ, par ni ngʼato ok nyal pimo pi gi lwet achiel.
Third Example Sentence: Jo ma otimo tich gi achiel ka achiel puonjore ni ngʼato ok nyal pimo pi gi lwet achiel.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal clause; contrastive structure; copular omission typical in Dholuo
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Desire does not guarantee entitlement or inheritance
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Dwaro mari ok nyal miyo iwinjo ni en mari
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used in land and succession debates in Luo clans where entitlement is lineage-based
First Example Sentence: Ne odwaro lowo mar wuon gi, to jo dongo owacho ni dwaro ok en gi wadu.
Second Example Sentence: Kata ka icham gima ngʼato moko timo, dwaro ok en gi wadu.
Third Example Sentence: Ka jo ma wuoyo matek paroni, dwaro ok en gi wadu ema ibiro winjo.

Idiom (Dholuo): Yath mar athi ok nyal kelo pi
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative metaphor; noun phrase subject + modal verb
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A dry tree cannot produce water; an incapable person cannot deliver results
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ngʼato ma onge teko ok nyal miyo gima idwaro
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from rural ecological knowledge of seasonal drought in Luo regions
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Dholuo): [jàt̪ʰ mār àt̪ʰí ɔ̀k ɲàl kélò pì]
First Example Sentence: Gik ma wan gi e ofis nyisore ni yath mar athi ok nyal kelo pi.
Second Example Sentence: Ne ochopo mondo otim tich maduongʼ, to yath mar athi ok nyal kelo pi.
Third Example Sentence: Ka irito ngʼato ma onge rieko, yath mar athi ok nyal kelo pi.

Idiom (Dholuo): Wuod gi wuod ok nyal bedo gi piny achiel
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative clause; possessive construction; kinship metaphor
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Two strong leaders cannot share authority peacefully
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Jo ariyo ma gin gi teko ok nyal bedo e kar achiel gi konyruok maber
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common in clan leadership struggles and local chieftain narratives
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Dholuo): [wùɔ̀d̪ gí wùɔ̀d̪ ɔ̀k ɲàl bɛ̀dɔ̀ gí pìɲ àt͡ʃíɛ̀l]
First Example Sentence: E siasa waneno ni wuod gi wuod ok nyal bedo gi piny achiel.
Second Example Sentence: Ka jodongo ariyo paro ni gin duto gin gi teko, wuod gi wuod ok nyal bedo gi piny achiel.
Third Example Sentence: Puonj ni wuod gi wuod ok nyal bedo gi piny achiel omiyo ginyalo yiero jachungʼ achiel.

Idiom (Dholuo): Otero dhako e piny mar dho
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase idiom; transitive verb + locative phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To expose someone to public embarrassment
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Osekel ngʼato e wichkuot mar oganda
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to communal assemblies where disputes were aired publicly
First Example Sentence: Ne ok dwarore otero dhako e piny mar dho nikech wachno ne nyalo dhi e piny maber.
Second Example Sentence: Ka iluongo ngʼato e baraza mondo ilos wachne, otero dhako e piny mar dho ema itimo.
Third Example Sentence: Omondo gin yud yiero ka onge otero dhako e piny mar dho.

Idiom (Dholuo): Chuny gi wangʼ ok nyal wuok e yore ariyo
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound clause; coordinated noun subjects; modal negation
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One cannot emotionally commit to two conflicting sides
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ngʼato ok nyal yiero gik ariyo ma opogore e kare achiel
Usage Contexts: Romantic relationships, political loyalty, moral discourse
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Frequently used in courtship songs and modern political speeches
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Dholuo): [t͡ʃúɲ gí wàŋ ɔ̀k ɲàl wùɔ̀k ɛ̀ jɔ̀rɛ̀ àríjɔ̀]
First Example Sentence: Ne gin gi chuny gi wangʼ e kar ariyo, to chuny gi wangʼ ok nyal wuok e yore ariyo.
Second Example Sentence: Ka iparo yiero ariyo, par ni chuny gi wangʼ ok nyal wuok e yore ariyo.
Third Example Sentence: Wach mar hera nyiso ni chuny gi wangʼ ok nyal wuok e yore ariyo.

Idiom (Dholuo): Lwet ma ok otemo ok nyal ngʼeyo ber mar pi
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative clause structure; negative participial modifier
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Experience is necessary to understand value
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ngʼato ma ok osebedo gi tich ok nyal ngʼeyo berne
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Emerges from fishing and farming practices requiring hands-on labor
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Dholuo): [lwɛ̀t̪ mā ɔ̀k ɔ̀t̪émɔ̀ ɔ̀k ɲàl ŋéjɔ̀ bɛ̀r mār pì]
First Example Sentence: Puonjore ni lwet ma ok otemo ok nyal ngʼeyo ber mar pi.
Second Example Sentence: Nyithindo manyien puonjore ni lwet ma ok otemo ok nyal ngʼeyo ber mar pi.
Third Example Sentence: Ka itiyo ka itiyo, ibiro ngʼeyo ni lwet ma ok otemo ok nyal ngʼeyo ber mar pi.

Idiom (Dholuo): Wangʼ ma ler ok luwo mudho
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative clause idiom; adjectival modifier; negative verb
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A clear conscience does not fear scrutiny
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ngʼato ma ok timo rach ok luoro penjo
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Often cited in church sermons across Luo-speaking regions
First Example Sentence: Ne owinjo penjo mangʼeny to wangʼ ma ler ok luwo mudho.
Second Example Sentence: Ka icham ni isetimo maber, wangʼ ma ler ok luwo mudho.
Third Example Sentence: E ot mar ngima, wangʼ ma ler ok luwo mudho ema puonj.

Idiom (Dholuo): Pi ma ochungʼ ok nyal doko nam
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative clause metaphor; stative verb; negative modal
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ngʼato ma ok nyalo medruok ok nyal doko maduongʼ
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws from Lake Victoria imagery where flowing water symbolizes vitality
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Dholuo): [pì mā ɔ̀t͡ʃúŋ ɔ̀k ɲàl dɔ̀kɔ̀ nàm]
First Example Sentence: Japuonj owacho ni pi ma ochungʼ ok nyal doko nam ka ne opuonjo jothurwa.
Second Example Sentence: Ka idwaro medruok e tich, par ni pi ma ochungʼ ok nyal doko nam.
Third Example Sentence: Oganda ma ok loyo yore manyien ni pi ma ochungʼ ok nyal doko nam.
